title: GitHub Copilot
description: GitHub Copilot architecture, usage patterns, enterprise administration, and best practices. Understand how Copilot constructs context, ranks suggestions, and integrates into developer workflows.

sections.title: Getting Started
sections.description: Install the Copilot extension in VS Code, JetBrains, or Neovim. Authenticate with your GitHub account. Start typing and receive inline suggestions from the Copilot model trained on public code.
sections.codeBlock:
# Installation commands
# VS Code:
ext install github.copilot
ext install github.copilot-chat

# Neovim (lazy.nvim):
{
  "github/copilot.vim",
  event = "InsertEnter",
  config = function()
    vim.keymap.set('i', '<C-J>', 'copilot#Accept("<CR>")', { expr = true })
  end
}

# JetBrains:
# Settings → Plugins → Marketplace → search "GitHub Copilot"

# Keyboard shortcuts:
# Tab: accept suggestion
# Alt/Option + ]: next suggestion
# Alt/Option + [: previous suggestion
# Ctrl + Enter: open suggestions panel

sections.title: Usage Patterns
sections.description: Copilot works best with clear intent signals: descriptive function names, type annotations, docstrings, and well-structured imports. It excels at boilerplate, tests, and algorithmic code.

sections.title: Enterprise Admin
sections.description: Enterprise administrators manage Copilot via the GitHub Enterprise settings. Assign seats, configure telemetry, manage content exclusions, and review usage metrics through the admin dashboard.
sections.codeBlock:
# Enterprise setup checklist
# 1. Enable Copilot in Enterprise settings
# Settings → Enterprise → Policies → Copilot
#
# 2. Content exclusions (public code matches):
# Settings → Copilot → Content exclusions
# Add repos whose code should not suggest
#
# 3. Seat assignment:
gh api \
  /enterprises/{enterprise}/copilot/billing/seats \
  --method POST \
  --field selected_usernames='["user1","user2"]'
#
# 4. Audit log:
# Settings → Enterprise → Audit log
# Filter: copilot._
#
# 5. Telemetry:
# Copilot chat history (opt-in)
# Code suggestion acceptance rate
# Language breakdown reports

sections.title: Best Practices
sections.description: Write clear function signatures, keep functions focused, provide type annotations, and use meaningful names. Review every suggestion — Copilot can produce plausible but incorrect code.
sections.codeBlock:
# DO: be explicit
async function calculateInvoiceTotal(
  items: LineItem[],
  taxRate: number,
  discount?: Coupon
): Promise<Money> {
  // Copilot generates accurate implementation
}

# DON'T: vague names
async function doThing(a: any, b: any): Promise<any>

# Prompt engineering tips:
# - Write the function signature first
# - Add type annotations (TypeScript, Python types)
# - Write a comment describing the next few lines
# - Use well-named variables in scope
# - Check suggestions for security (SQL injection, etc.)
#
# /fix in Copilot Chat: ask to fix issues
# /explain: understand generated code
# /tests: generate test cases

sections.title: Limitations & Tips
sections.description: Copilot has a limited context window (~2-4K tokens), cannot execute code, may produce insecure patterns, and performs poorly on niche or internal APIs. It's a productivity accelerator, not a replacement for code review.
sections.codeBlock:
# Known limitations
# - Context window: ~2048 tokens (file prefix + suffix + imports + similar snippets)
# - No awareness of project-wide architecture
# - May suggest deprecated or insecure APIs
# - Struggles with:
#   * Internal/private package APIs
#   * Domain-specific algorithms
#   * Very long files (>500 lines)
#   * Cross-file refactoring
#
# Mitigation strategies:
# 1. Break large files into focused modules
# 2. Keep related code close together
# 3. Add JSDoc/docstrings for public APIs
# 4. Use Copilot Chat for multi-file changes
# 5. Always review for security: injection, auth, secrets
#
# Prompting Copilot Chat:
# @workspace /explain "How does auth work?"
# /fix "This doesn't handle edge cases"
# /tests "Generate unit tests for this function"

details: GitHub Copilot is powered by OpenAI Codex, a transformer model fine-tuned on natural language and public code. On each keystroke, the Copilot extension assembles a context window containing the current file prefix (code before cursor), suffix (code after cursor), surrounding function or class boundary, imports and resolved types, and nearest-neighbor snippets found via locality-sensitive hashing (LSH) over the open-source training corpus. The model generates up to 10 candidate completions. These pass through a multi-stage filter: deduplication via edit distance thresholds, syntactic validity via AST parsing, and relevance ranking by perplexity score. The top 1-3 suggestions are presented inline. Enterprise administrators control seat assignment, content exclusions (repos whose code should not appear in suggestions), and telemetry collection. Copilot Chat extends this with conversational context, enabling /fix, /explain, /tests, and @workspace commands for multi-file reasoning.
